text:p.text:span: Tablet typology: both are compilation tablets (Type I).
saying (the words of) Åu-Sin, your king:
The envoy whom I sent to you, … above and beyond …
At daybreak my orders that I had provided, to me … you were not to exceed.
As for me, whatever (you) say ­to me …
After you set out for to the center of the frontier territory,
you approached the territory and after spending the night …
Their nobles then staged an ambush against you.
you will not be able to root them out from their hiding places.
When their cities have been handed over,
until they come out from these fortifications, let no one get through.
Once their (fighting) men had been killed,
their women/workers of the citi(es) of your territory, who had been­ captured, were not to be [harmed
Why did you not act in accordance with my instructions?
But although it was not (normally) in your power to execute and blind people, nor to destroy cities,
When earlier <my> father Åulgi had built ­
The throne is mine, it ­not the throne of Åulgi!
is to come to you and
He is to send them [from among his own conscripts].
that I ­have already given to him, is to build the fortifications.
But you, with the workers that have been entrusted to you—dig ­the moat!
to do nothing that would alter the allegiance of the frontier territory;
neither you can demobilize the troops!
Then, have envoys bring news of­ the territory to me each morning!

The only two sources for this letter are of unknown origin and both contain mistakes, omissions, and unorthodox writings and therefore all precise grammatical analysis must be taken with a grain of salt. Needless to say the interpretation of this letter is highly conjectural. The text of this letter was also known to the compiler of the
. The scribe of X2 may have written
, and the passage may have read
is documented in lexical texts (the variant
in X2 is inexplicable to me); see
. The Akkadian word means both “dwelling” and “ambush, military post,” but the Sumerian loan only encompasses the latter meaning. See, for example,
, “the Amorites sprung an armed ambush against me.”
. These lines were translated differently in

B 53a: “its cities, it borders, and finally right up to its walls—they climbed up, indeed they got over.” Because of the fragmentary nature of this passage it is not clear just where the previous royal instructions begin. My translation is based on the understanding that Åu-Sin had ordered Åarrum-bani to concentrate on building rather than on fighting, as the province had already been pacified, and for the time being the conquered hostiles could not break through into Babylonia.

require a full treatment; the meanings cover the conceptual range from “trust” to “authority.” It is possible that this, and this passage should be understood as “Although you were not­ permitted to execute and
blind people nor to destroy cities, (other than that) I gave you (full) authority.” Compare
, “One Given Authority/Trust by Enlil, King of Ur” (part of a long name bestowed on the monarch by Enlil).

makes no ­sense, and the one text that preserves this line is clearly corrupt. The scribe had problems both with the content and the layout of the text, and had to squeeze it into the line, which may also account for the omission of the pronoun on
. One may suggest two emendations. The first possibility ­is that this is a confused writing of
, the ­name used in other CKU letters for the fortifications built by Åulgi. The scribe who wrote X2 regularly abbreviated proper names (
and left it at that. The grammatical case of the Akkadian word is a problem, nevertheless. Note that this is the only direct evidence, outside ­of the
, that Åu-Sin was Åulgi’s son (but see D. I. Owen,

15 [2001] 19–20). One should also consider another possibility: that the confusion stemmed from the name of another player in the CKU, namely Puzur-Åulgi. Faced with a badly written original, the scribe may have assumed that this was the name of Åarrum-bani’s father, and may have understood this all as
, “when earlier Puzur-Åulgi, your father, had worked on the wall….” In view of what follows, I have chosen the first interpretation.
, “y’all,” is rare; the proper form seems to be
240/267). In the last example the referent is dual. The only other example of
known to me refers to more than two:
za-e-en-zé-en diå-àm åu te-ba-ab-zé-en Ñá-e dili-Ñu
, “(the fox said:) ‘you all are nine, take one (sheep); I as I am

Vers. B 74: 5–6). It is possible that Sumerian at one time distinguished between “y’all” and “you two,” but that distinction, if it existed, would probably have been lost on the OB scribe of this text. Whatever the case, the referent remains elusive, as up to this point the words of the king were directed to one person, Åarrum-bani.
The only source for this passage has
in 29 and 30; in light of the many irregularities of this text, it is possible that it stands for
and that other versions may turn up that have it as “Is my throne not the throne of Åulgi?”
may be simply a mistake for
. The translation is based on the assumption that it stands for
. It is also possible that this is to be interpreted as “it is my throne,” and the previous libe as “none of you were there.”
does not usually refer to individuals, and therefore it is possible that the
and that the translation should be: “he will bring one army regiment.” The rendition used here is based on the analogy with the only other use of a number before
that I am aware of, in the
, “Attamanum brought (a contingent of) six hundred soldiers of Gungunum into Edana.”

office:text.text:p: I cannot explain the double genitive or genitive and locative in this line.

text:p.text:span: makes absolutely no sense; I have provisionally understood this as a garbled form of
. Steve Tinney suggests to me that this may be a visual error from a badly written original:
. These lines are addressed to both Åarrum-bani and Babati (or perhaps Lu-Nanna), like the end of ÅAr1 (2), which is directed at both Aradmu and Apila
must be an imperative form of
­ is a mistake brought about by the association with ­
3 98. For a moat associated with fortifications see, e.g.,
åà iti min-kam-ma-ka-àm gú íd-dur-ùl-ka-ta bàd
, “in the course of two months he constructed Dur-Samsu-iluna (‘Fort Samsu-iluna’) on the bank of the Diyala and dug its moat.”
is undoubtedly a syllabic rendering of
, and is only attested in X2, which is hardly surprising.
makes no sense, and the context strongly suggests that this is a visual mistake for
, “from now on bring me your news (continuously).”
